title: Update the textbooks: There’s another way to make DNA
html_body:
Today in Science and science: A burst in human evolution 10,000 years ago, warming waters threaten big sharks, and more
Brought to you by Danaher Corporation
View this email in your browser
17 April 2026
Today’s Visualized examines the photographer behind this week’s Science cover photo. But first, catch up on the latest science news, including a new way to make DNA and an ancient burst of human evolution.
Climate Change  |  Science
Sharks in hot water
Shark Week is still months away, but it’s never the wrong time to focus on these iconic predators—and the challenges they face. New research published in Science finds that warming waters may hurt these ocean rulers due to their unique metabolisms.

Many shark species are mesothermic, meaning they run warmer than the surrounding seawater, but not quite as hot as warm-blooded creatures. To study this rare group of fishes, scientists analyzed biologging data to see how heat traveled between their bodies and their surroundings.

The team found that species including the great white shark and the basking shark burned nearly four times more energy than cold-blooded fishes of the same size. That’s partly because large fishes generate heat faster than they lose it to the water. The researchers also created theoretical “heat-balance thresholds” to assess the temperatures above which such animals can’t keep their own body heat stable. In these conditions—which look increasingly likely with global warming—mesotherms will need to slow down, change their blood circulation, or move into deeper, cooler waters, all of which could affect how they hunt.

“Today’s oceans are changing at unprecedented speeds,” lead author Nicholas Payne told Inside Climate News. “The alarm bells are ringing loudly at this point.”
Read the paper
Cell Biology  |  News from Science
Update the textbooks: There’s another way to make DNA
In a newly discovered bacterial defense system, paired strands of DNA (orange and cyan) are synthesized by two enzymes: One (yellow) uses an RNA template (beige) to guide the assembly of the nucleotide bases that make up DNA, while a second, highly unusual enzyme (light blue) uses its own amino acids as a template.  Hyunbin Lee
Current textbooks say that new DNA made in cells comes from copying a template—in other words, an existing double helix must be unwound so that a polymerase enzyme can read each single strand to build a complementary partner and end up with two copies of the original DNA. But yesterday in Science, a team described a bacterial enzyme that synthesizes DNA without a nucleic acid template, using its own structure as a guide. “ The research is groundbreaking,” said biochemist Philip Kranzusch, who studies bacterial defenses.

“Pretty cool!” added Adi Millman, a computational biologist. The use of a protein as a template for DNA synthesis, she said, “is a meaningful conceptual shift from the classical central dogma,” in which information flows in one direction from nucleic acids like DNA to protein. Inspired by how the powerful genome editor CRISPR was developed from another bacterial defense system, scientists hope the novel form of DNA synthesis can be turned into a tool for basic biological research or a better way to make DNA for biotech uses.
read the Science paper
Read the Full Story
Genetics  |  News from Science
Ancient human DNA reveals “massive” genetic shifts tied to rise of farming, wheels, and metal tools
The human genome hasn’t been as static recently as evolutionary biologists thought. By analyzing nearly 16,000 ancient human genomes from Europe and the Middle East, researchers measured human genetic change over 18,000 years and found hundreds of genetic shifts across Europe’s population in a relatively short time. “ It’s a powerful new approach to detecting natural selection from ancient DNA,” said evolutionary geneticist Iain Mathieson.

For example, the analysis, published this week in Nature, found new signs that natural selection nudged traits such as tuberculosis resistance and lower body fat to become more common in western Eurasians within the past 10,000 years or so. The work challenges previous studies of human evolution, based largely on analyzing the DNA of modern people, that had concluded our genomes were relatively stable over the past tens of thousands of years. Geneticist David Reich, an ancient DNA pioneer, led the work and said, “This is the most important work I have been involved in for a decade. … It is finally realizing the promise of ancient DNA to reveal as much about biology as history.”
Read the full story
SPONSORED
Danaher combines AI-driven discovery with a proven strategic framework to expand what’s possible for the future of healthcare.
Read Now
Visualized
Capturing a changing landscape in a lifetime
Emily Petersen, Photography Managing Editor, Science
The Colorado River meanders through the Grand Canyon under a full moon. Evidence shows that the signature of sands from the Bidahochi basin (southeast of this photo’s location) matches that of ancestral Colorado River deposits. The arrival of the river there 6.6 million years ago marks a previously unconfirmed step in the integration of the river-canyon system, which set in motion the processes that shaped an iconic landscape.  Photo: PETE MCBRIDE
 
Science has published several of photojournalist Pete McBride’s pictures. His grand views of busy national park landscapes, memorable perspectives, and   thoughtful compositions always leave us feeling reinspired and astonished that there is still so much to learn about rivers and waterways. This is why Science Senior Photo Editor Charlie Borst worked with McBride again for this week’s cover: a quiet moonlit moment of the Colorado River in the Grand Canyon. McBride’s photograph is a beautiful representation of the Colorado River carving the Grand Canyon and the close relationship between these two great forces. It was a perfect perspective to visualize He et al.’s research paper in this issue.
The light slips through the slot canyon and reflects off the water in North Canyon within the Grand Canyon.  PETE MCBRIDE
 
Last month, McBride made the jump to writing with his new book A Witness to Water. I found the book to be a vivid firsthand perspective on the changes to the Colorado River that only a photographer could capture. McBride has spent his career documenting the Colorado, and his expert knowledge, daring adventures, and personal transformation leave the reader with a deeper understanding of and connection to what feels like an all-too-familiar topic to Science readers: how climate change is impacting our planet. McBride delivers on a compelling read for anyone curious about how a photojournalist can transform into an expert witness for a rapidly changing landscape.
McBride sits behind his dad in a Cessna 180 as they scout for photo opportunities along the Colorado River.  Pete McBride
 
REad the NEWS FROM SCIENCE STORY
Read the paper
podcast
How to keep quantum computers cool, whether prediction markets harm public health, and podcasting on podcasting
By SARAH CRESPI, Zack Savitsky   |   16 April 2026
On this week’s show: New tech for keeping quantum computers cool without depending on a rare helium isotope, the potential harms of commercialized prediction markets, and how podcasting changed a Ph.D. student’s life.
Listen Now
Et Cetera
Sex on the brain
A study of more than a million brain cells finds more than 100 genes where expression differs between male and female brains. Though, neuroscientists were quick to note that these make up less than 1% of the variation in gene expression across the cells. “Having these gene expression signatures provides a molecular handle to understanding the biology of how the brains of men and women might be functioning slightly differently in the context of the different hormonal environments that their bodies produce,” said one expert. “Understanding sex differences in disease susceptibility could lead to better treatments to benefit everyone.”
Science Paper and RELATED PERSPECTIVE  |  Read more at Nature
Alzheimer’s drugs not ‘clinically meaningful’
A new Cochrane review of anti-amyloid drugs finds their benefits are negligible: “Successful removal of amyloid from the brain does not seem to be associated with clinically meaningful effects in people with mild cognitive impairment or mild dementia due to Alzheimer’s disease  .” However, some experts find the review’s methods wanting. “The problem with the review is the mix of ingredients,” said one. “They took some of the rotten ingredients and mixed it in with the fresh food, and the result is a stinky stew.”
Cochrane Review  |  Read more at The New York Times
Got milk? (and a hot tub)
In the first of seven congressional hearings scheduled, Secretary of Health and Human Services Robert F. Kennedy, Jr. tried to focus on his efforts to improve nutrition and fight chronic disease—but many were more interested in talking about NIH funding cuts and vaccines. “You’re spending taxpayer dollars to drink milk shirtless in a hot tub with Kid Rock,” said Representative Linda Sanchez (D–CA). “Somehow you think that’s a better public health message than informing the public about the benefits of vaccines. Really?”
Read more at Scientific American
Last but not least
I never would have expected a feature on Finnish refrigerators to be so fascinating; I have to give kudos to Zack Savitsky for making such a seemingly dull topic absolutely engrossing.
Christie Wilcox, Editor, ScienceAdviser

With contributions from Hannah Richter and John Travis
